items.prompt: Which heavyweight boxing champion, born Cassius Clay, refused induction into the U.S. military during the Vietnam War and famously declared he could 'float like a butterfly, sting like a bee'?
items.explanation:
Heavyweight boxer, American (1942–2016), born Cassius Marcellus Clay Jr.
Won the heavyweight championship three times (1964, 1974, 1978)—first fighter to do so
Won Olympic light-heavyweight gold at the 1960 Rome Games as an 18-year-old
Refused Vietnam War draft in 1967, citing religious beliefs; stripped of his title and banned from boxing for three years
Defeated George Foreman in the "Rumble in the Jungle" (Kinshasa, 1974) using the rope-a-dope strategy
Lit the Olympic cauldron at the 1996 Atlanta Games, visibly trembling from Parkinson's disease
Changed his name after converting to Islam and joining the Nation of Islam in 1964
items.cardFront: Boxing, 3x heavyweight champion, 'float like a butterfly'

links.text: Boxing, 3x heavyweight champion, 'float like a butterfly'

items.prompt: Which basketball player won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls, earned five MVP awards, and became synonymous with a Nike sneaker brand bearing his name?
items.explanation:
Basketball, American (b. 1963), played for the Chicago Bulls and Washington Wizards
Won six NBA championships (1991–1993, 1996–1998) and six Finals MVP awards with the Bulls
Five-time NBA regular-season MVP; 14-time All-Star; 10-time scoring champion
Cut from his high school varsity basketball team as a sophomore—used it as lifelong motivation
Nike's Air Jordan brand became a multi-billion-dollar empire; debuted with the banned Air Jordan 1 in 1985
Starred in *Space Jam* (1996); ESPN's *The Last Dance* (2020) documented his final Bulls season
Briefly retired to play minor-league baseball (1993–1995) before returning with the two-word fax: "I'm back"
items.cardFront: Basketball, 6 NBA titles with Chicago Bulls, Nike brand

links.text: Basketball, 6 NBA titles with Chicago Bulls, Nike brand

items.prompt: Which tennis player won 23 Grand Slam singles titles—the most in the Open Era—after learning the sport from her father on public courts in Compton, California?
items.explanation:
Tennis, American (b. 1981)
23 Grand Slam singles titles—most in the Open Era, one short of Margaret Court's all-time record of 24
Trained by father Richard Williams on public courts in Compton, California alongside sister Venus
Won four consecutive Grand Slam titles across 2002–2003, nicknamed the "Serena Slam"
Held the world #1 ranking for 319 weeks total; won her 23rd major at the 2017 Australian Open while pregnant
Sister Venus also won seven Grand Slam singles titles; the pair won 14 Grand Slam doubles titles together
Retired from professional tennis in September 2022 after the U.S. Open
items.cardFront: Tennis, 23 Grand Slam singles titles (Open Era record)

items.prompt: Which Jamaican sprinter won eight Olympic gold medals and holds the world records in both the 100 meters (9.58s) and 200 meters (19.19s)?
items.explanation:
Sprinting, Jamaican (b. 1986), nicknamed "Lightning Bolt"
Holds world records in the 100m (9.58s, Berlin 2009) and 200m (19.19s, Berlin 2009)
Won eight Olympic gold medals across three Games: 100m, 200m, and 4×100m at each of Beijing 2008, London 2012, and Rio 2016
Completed an unprecedented "triple triple"—three consecutive golds in the same three events
At 6’5″, considered unusually tall for a sprinter; his long stride was a biomechanical advantage
Celebrated wins with the signature "Lightning Bolt" pose (left arm extended skyward)
Retired after the 2017 World Championships in London; briefly pursued a professional soccer career

items.prompt: Which Brazilian soccer player won three FIFA World Cups (1958, 1962, and 1970)—the only player ever to achieve that feat—and scored roughly 1,200 career goals across official and unofficial matches?
items.explanation:
Soccer/football, Brazilian (1940–2022), born Edson Arantes do Nascimento
Only player to win three FIFA World Cup titles: 1958 (age 17), 1962, and 1970 with Brazil
Scored 77 goals in 92 international appearances for Brazil
Career goal total disputed: ~1,281 per FIFA (including friendlies), ~767 in official matches
Played for Santos FC (1956–1974) and finished his career with the New York Cosmos (1975–1977)
Scored his 1,000th career goal on November 19, 1969 (a penalty vs. Vasco da Gama at the Maracanã)
Declared a national treasure by Brazil's government, which blocked his transfer to European clubs
items.cardFront: Soccer, 3 World Cups with Brazil, disputed ~1,200 goals

links.text: Soccer, 3 World Cups with Brazil, disputed ~1,200 goals

items.prompt: Which baseball player hit 714 career home runs, was sold from the Red Sox to the Yankees in a deal that supposedly cursed Boston for 86 years, and allegedly 'called his shot' in the 1932 World Series?
items.explanation:
Baseball, American (1895–1948), born George Herman Ruth Jr.
714 career home runs—a record that stood until Hank Aaron broke it in 1974
Sold from the Boston Red Sox to the New York Yankees in 1920 for $100,000; the Red Sox didn't win a World Series again until 2004 (the "Curse of the Bambino")
Hit 60 home runs in 1927, a single-season record that stood until Roger Maris hit 61 in 1961
Allegedly pointed to center field before homering in Game 3 of the 1932 World Series (the "called shot")
Began his career as a dominant left-handed pitcher for the Red Sox before converting to outfield
One of the first five inductees into the Baseball Hall of Fame (1936)
items.cardFront: Baseball, 714 home runs, 'Curse of the Bambino'

links.text: Baseball, 714 home runs, 'Curse of the Bambino'

items.prompt: Which ice hockey player, nicknamed 'The Great One,' held 61 NHL records at retirement, scored 2,857 career points, and had his #99 jersey retired across the entire league?
items.explanation:
Ice hockey, Canadian (b. 1961), nicknamed "The Great One"
Held 61 NHL records at retirement (1999); still holds over 50, including 2,857 career points and 1,963 assists
Career goals record of 894 stood for 26 years until Alexander Ovechkin surpassed it in 2025
His assists alone (1,963) would make him the all-time points leader over any other player
#99 retired league-wide by the NHL in 1999—the only number so honored
Scored 50 goals in just 39 games during the 1981–82 season (the standard benchmark is 50 in 50)
Won four Stanley Cups with the Edmonton Oilers (1984–1988); his 1988 trade to the Los Angeles Kings is called "The Trade" in Canada
items.cardFront: Ice hockey, 2,857 career points, #99 retired league-wide

links.text: Ice hockey, 2,857 career points, #99 retired league-wide

items.prompt: Which American track and field athlete won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics, undermining Nazi claims of Aryan racial superiority, and set or tied four world records in 45 minutes at a 1935 college meet?
items.explanation:
Track and field, American (1913–1980), born James Cleveland Owens
Won four gold medals at the 1936 Berlin Olympics (100m, 200m, long jump, 4×100m relay)—rebuking Nazi racial ideology
At the 1935 Big Ten Championships in Ann Arbor, tied one world record and set three more in approximately 45 minutes despite a back injury
His long jump record of 8.13m (set in 1935) stood for 25 years
Despite Olympic fame, returned to a segregated America; President Roosevelt never invited him to the White House
Worked as a gas station attendant and ran against horses in exhibitions to earn money after the Olympics
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously by President Ford in 1976
items.cardFront: Track and field, 4 golds at 1936 Berlin Olympics

links.text: Track and field, 4 golds at 1936 Berlin Olympics

items.prompt: Which swimmer holds the all-time records for both Olympic gold medals (23) and total Olympic medals (28), including an unprecedented eight golds at a single Games in 2008?
items.explanation:
Swimming, American (b. 1985)
23 Olympic gold medals and 28 total Olympic medals—both all-time records for any athlete in any sport
Won eight gold medals at the 2008 Beijing Olympics, surpassing Mark Spitz's record of seven at a single Games
Competed in five consecutive Olympics (2000–2016), debuting at age 15 in Sydney
Set 39 world records over his career across butterfly, individual medley, and freestyle events
Diagnosed with ADHD as a child; his coach Bob Bowman channeled his energy into swimming starting at age 11
Has spoken publicly about depression and suicidal thoughts after retirement, becoming a mental health advocate
items.cardFront: Swimming, 23 Olympic golds, 28 total Olympic medals

links.text: Swimming, 23 Olympic golds, 28 total Olympic medals

items.prompt: Which NFL quarterback won seven Super Bowls—the most by any player in history—after being selected 199th overall in the sixth round of the 2000 NFL Draft?
items.explanation:
NFL quarterback, American (b. 1977)
Seven Super Bowl victories—six with the New England Patriots, one with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers—more than any player in NFL history
Picked 199th overall in the 6th round of the 2000 draft; six quarterbacks were selected before him
Five-time Super Bowl MVP and three-time regular-season MVP
Played until age 45, retiring after the 2022 season as the NFL's all-time leader in passing yards (89,214) and touchdown passes (649)
Briefly retired in February 2022, then un-retired 40 days later before finally retiring for good in 2023
Won his seventh Super Bowl with the Buccaneers in 2021 at age 43, defeating Patrick Mahomes's Chiefs
items.cardFront: NFL, 7 Super Bowl wins, 199th draft pick

links.text: NFL, 7 Super Bowl wins, 199th draft pick

items.prompt: Which gymnast, the most decorated in history with 30+ world and Olympic medals combined, withdrew from events at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics citing 'the twisties' before returning triumphantly at the 2024 Paris Games?
items.explanation:
Gymnastics, American (b. 1997)
Most decorated gymnast in history: 11 Olympic medals (7 gold) and 30 World Championship medals (23 gold)
Won four golds and a bronze at the 2016 Rio Olympics in her Olympic debut
Withdrew from five of six events at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics citing "the twisties"—a mental block causing loss of spatial awareness mid-air
Returned at the 2024 Paris Olympics, winning three golds (team, all-around, vault) and a silver (floor)
Has four gymnastics skills named after her, including the Yurchenko double pike vault
Spent time in foster care as a child before being raised by her maternal grandparents in Houston
items.cardFront: Gymnastics, most decorated ever, 'the twisties' at Tokyo

links.text: Gymnastics, most decorated ever, 'the twisties' at Tokyo

items.prompt: Which baseball player broke Major League Baseball's color barrier in 1947 when he debuted for the Brooklyn Dodgers, and whose #42 was later retired across all MLB teams?
items.explanation:
Baseball, American (1919–1972), born Jack Roosevelt Robinson
Broke MLB's color barrier on April 15, 1947 with the Brooklyn Dodgers, ending six decades of segregation in the major leagues
Recruited by Dodgers general manager Branch Rickey, who required Robinson to endure racial abuse without retaliation for two years
Won Rookie of the Year in 1947 and NL MVP in 1949; six-time All-Star
#42 retired across all of MLB in 1997—the only number universally retired; April 15 is Jackie Robinson Day
Before baseball, was the first UCLA athlete to letter in four sports (baseball, basketball, football, track)
Inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in 1962
items.cardFront: Baseball, broke MLB color barrier 1947, #42 retired league-wide

links.text: Baseball, broke MLB color barrier 1947, #42 retired league-wide

items.prompt: Which Swiss tennis player won 20 Grand Slam singles titles, held the world #1 ranking for a record 237 consecutive weeks, and was widely admired for his elegant, fluid playing style?
items.explanation:
Tennis, Swiss (b. 1981), retired 2022
20 Grand Slam singles titles, including a record eight Wimbledon titles
Held the ATP #1 ranking for 237 consecutive weeks (2004–2008)—a record in any era of professional tennis
Known for an exceptionally fluid, graceful style; mastered every surface but was dominant on grass
Rivaled Rafael Nadal and Novak Djokovic in one of sport's greatest three-way rivalries
Won at least one Grand Slam title in each year from 2003 to 2010
Retired at the 2022 Laver Cup in London, playing his final match as a doubles partner with rival Nadal
Estimated career prize money exceeded $130 million; off-court earnings far surpassed that
items.cardFront: Tennis, 20 Grand Slams, 237 consecutive weeks at #1

links.text: Tennis, 20 Grand Slams, 237 consecutive weeks at #1

items.prompt: Which gymnast became the first in Olympic history to score a perfect 10.0, at the 1976 Montreal Games at age 14, when the scoreboard famously displayed 1.00 because it could not show her actual score?
items.explanation:
Gymnastics, Romanian (b. 1961)
First gymnast to score a perfect 10.0 in Olympic competition, on the uneven bars at the 1976 Montreal Games
The Omega scoreboard could only display three digits and showed 1.00 instead of 10.00
Earned seven perfect 10s at the 1976 Olympics, winning three golds (all-around, uneven bars, balance beam)
Won two more golds at the 1980 Moscow Olympics (balance beam, floor exercise)
Defected from Communist Romania in 1989, weeks before the fall of Ceaușescu's regime, by walking across the border into Hungary
Married American gymnast Bart Conner in 1996; lives in Norman, Oklahoma

items.prompt: Which athlete won both the decathlon and pentathlon at the 1912 Olympics, had his medals stripped for having been paid to play semi-pro baseball, and was also a standout in professional football and baseball?
items.explanation:
Multi-sport athlete, American (1887–1953), of the Sac and Fox Nation (Native American name Wa-Tho-Huk, "Bright Path")
Won gold in both the decathlon and pentathlon at the 1912 Stockholm Olympics
Stripped of his medals in 1913 after it emerged he had been paid to play semi-pro baseball; medals restored as co-champion in 1983, then as sole winner in 2022
Also played six seasons of Major League Baseball and was the first president of what became the NFL
King Gustav V of Sweden told him: "You, sir, are the greatest athlete in the world"
Named the greatest athlete of the first half of the 20th century by the Associated Press in 1950
Died in poverty in 1953; AP later named him the greatest athlete of the entire 20th century in 1999
items.cardFront: Decathlon/pentathlon gold 1912, medals stripped then restored

links.text: Decathlon/pentathlon gold 1912, medals stripped then restored

items.prompt: Which tennis player defeated Bobby Riggs in the nationally televised 1973 'Battle of the Sexes' match watched by an estimated 90 million viewers, and won 12 Grand Slam singles titles while pioneering women's sports equality?
items.explanation:
Tennis, American (b. 1943)
Defeated Bobby Riggs 6–4, 6–3, 6–3 in the "Battle of the Sexes" at the Houston Astrodome on September 20, 1973
The match drew ~90 million TV viewers worldwide and 30,472 in-person spectators
12 Grand Slam singles titles, including six Wimbledon singles championships
Founded the Women's Tennis Association (WTA) in 1973 and fought for equal prize money
Co-founded the Women's Sports Foundation and *womenSports* magazine
The USTA National Tennis Center (home of the U.S. Open) was renamed the Billie Jean King National Tennis Center in 2006
Awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Obama in 2009
items.cardFront: Tennis, 'Battle of the Sexes' vs Riggs 1973

links.text: Tennis, 'Battle of the Sexes' vs Riggs 1973

items.prompt: Which Argentine soccer player scored both the 'Hand of God' goal and the 'Goal of the Century' in the same 1986 World Cup quarterfinal match against England, leading Argentina to that year's World Cup title?
items.explanation:
Soccer/football, Argentine (1960–2020)
Scored both the "Hand of God" (a handball the referee missed) and the "Goal of the Century" (a 60-yard solo dribble past five England players) in the same 1986 World Cup quarterfinal
Captained Argentina to the 1986 World Cup title in Mexico, winning the Golden Ball as the tournament's best player
Spent seven seasons at Napoli (1984–1991), leading the club to its only two Serie A titles (1987, 1990)
Banned from football for 15 months in 1991 after testing positive for cocaine; battled addiction throughout his life
Banned again during the 1994 World Cup for ephedrine; his wide-eyed celebration after scoring vs. Greece became an infamous image
Died of cardiac arrest at age 60; three days of national mourning declared in Argentina
items.cardFront: Soccer, 'Hand of God' and 'Goal of the Century' (1986)

links.text: Soccer, 'Hand of God' and 'Goal of the Century'

items.prompt: Which 7’1″ basketball player scored 100 points in a single NBA game on March 2, 1962—a record that still stands—and averaged 50.4 points per game for the entire 1961–62 season?
items.explanation:
Basketball, American (1936–1999), stood 7’1″
Scored 100 points for the Philadelphia Warriors vs. the New York Knicks on March 2, 1962 at Hershey, Pennsylvania—still the NBA single-game record
Averaged 50.4 points per game in the 1961–62 season—no other player has averaged even 45
Won two NBA championships (1967 Philadelphia 76ers, 1972 Los Angeles Lakers)
Led the league in scoring seven consecutive seasons and in rebounding 11 times
The only player to score 4,000 points in a single season (4,029 in 1961–62)
Claimed in his autobiography to have slept with 20,000 women—a figure widely cited but impossible to verify
Preferred the nickname "The Big Dipper" but was more commonly called "Wilt the Stilt," which he disliked
items.cardFront: Basketball, 100 points in one game (1962), 50.4 ppg season

links.text: Basketball, 100 points in one game, 50.4 ppg season
